1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from
10	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
15	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
16	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
17	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
18	derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
19	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
20	available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
21	over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
22	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
23	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
24	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
25	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
26	
27	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
28	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
29	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
30	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
31	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
32	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
33	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
34	\[
35	  \begin{array}{ccc}
36	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
37	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
38	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
39	  \end{array}
40	\]
41	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
42	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
43	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
44	
45	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
46	
47	\begin{definition}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
51	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
53	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
54	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
55	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
56	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
57	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
58	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
59	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
60	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
61	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
62	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
63	  % the unit ideal, see
64	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
65	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
66	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
67	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
68	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
69	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
70	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
71	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
72	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
73	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
74	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
75	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
76	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
77	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
78	  \[
79	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
80	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
81	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
82	  \]
83	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
84	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
85	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
86	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
87	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
88	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
89	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
90	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
91	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
92	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
93	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
94	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
95	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
96	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
97	\end{definition}
98	
99	\begin{definition}
100	
101	\leanok
102	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
103	  \label{def:cech_complex}
104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
105	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
106	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
107	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
108	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
109	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
110	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
111	  % is identified with the complex
112	  % $$
113	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
114	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
115	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
116	  % \ldots
117	  % $$
118	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
119	  % \begin{equation}
120	  % \label{equation-extended}
121	  % 0 \to
122	  % M \to
123	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
124	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
125	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
126	  % \ldots
127	  % \end{equation}
128	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
129	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."

131	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
132	  vanishing).}
133	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
134	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
135	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
136	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
137	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
138	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
139	  in degree \(p\) it is
140	  \[
141	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
142	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
143	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
144	  \]
145	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
146	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
147	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
148	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
149	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
150	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
151	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
152	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
153	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
154	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
155	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
156	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
157	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
158	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
159	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
160	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
161	\end{definition}
162	
163	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
164	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
165	
166	It is convenient to factor the construction of
167	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
168	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the entire mathematical
169	content that is not yet available off the shelf is isolated in the middle piece.
170	
171	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
172	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
173	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
174	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
175	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
176	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
177	\[
178	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
179	    \;=\;
180	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
181	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
182	\]
183	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
184	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
185	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
186	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
187	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
188	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
189	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
190	
191	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
192	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
193	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
194	\[
195	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
196	  \qquad
197	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
198	\]
199	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
200	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
201	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
202	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
203	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
204	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
205	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
206	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
207	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
208	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
209	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
210	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
211	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
212	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
213	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
214	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. This
215	functoriality is \emph{not} coupled to the project's tensor--pullback substrate;
216	it consumes only Mathlib's pseudofunctor packaging of pullback and pushforward.
217	Concretely, pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
218	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
219	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
220	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
221	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
222	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts about this
223	packaging: the mate identity expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback
224	comparison as the pushforward comparison (the lemma
225	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}), its identity normalisation
226	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackId\_hom}, and the pseudofunctor unitor and pentagon
227	(associativity) coherences \texttt{pseudofunctor\_left\_unitality},
228	\texttt{pseudofunctor\_right\_unitality}, \texttt{pseudofunctor\_associativity}.
229	All of these are already available off the shelf, so the functoriality of the
230	relative-direct-image functor is a \emph{consumer of Mathlib's coherence},
231	independent of the project-local sheafification/pullback machinery of the
232	tensor--pullback substrate. The laws are nonetheless not formal trivialities: each
233	is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison isomorphisms along
234	the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and past the adjunction
235	unit, gluing them across two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions; the obstacle is the
236	bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
237	
238	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
239	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
240	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
241	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
242	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
243	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
244	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
245	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
246	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
247	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
248	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
249	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
250	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
251	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
252	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
253	that content is the pushforward/pullback pseudofunctor coherence supplied by
254	Mathlib (the comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
255	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
256	and their unitor/pentagon identities), not any project-local ingredient.
257	
258	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
259	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
260	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
261	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
262	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
263	
264	\begin{definition}\leanok
265	[Arrow of an affine cover]
266	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
268	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
269	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
270	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
271	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
272	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
273	  apply.
274	\end{definition}
275	
276	\begin{definition}\leanok
277	[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
278	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
279	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve}
280	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
281	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
282	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
283	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
284	  \[
285	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
286	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
287	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
288	  \]
289	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
290	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
291	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
292	\end{definition}
293	
294	\begin{definition}\leanok
295	[Push--pull object map]
296	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
298	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
299	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
300	  \[
301	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
302	  \]
303	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
304	\end{definition}
305	
306	\begin{definition}\leanok
307	[Push--pull comparison map]
308	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap}
310	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
311	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
312	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
313	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
314	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
315	  \[
316	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
317	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
318	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
319	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
320	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
321	      \;=\;
322	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
323	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
324	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
325	  \]
326	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
327	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
328	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
329	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
330	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
331	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
332	\end{definition}

334	\begin{lemma}
335	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
336	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
337	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
338	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
339	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
340	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
341	\end{lemma}
342	
343	\begin{proof}
344	  A statement purely about the pushforward/pullback comparison isomorphisms, with no
345	  sectionwise content. Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward
346	  comparison as the conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison
347	  (\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}); the identity leg then collapses by the
348	  identity normalisation \texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackId\_hom} together with the right
349	  unitality \texttt{pseudofunctor\_right\_unitality}, and the two over-triangle
350	  \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
351	\end{proof}
352	
353	\begin{lemma}
354	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
355	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
356	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp}
357	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
358	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
359	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
360	  \[
361	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
362	  \]
363	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
364	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
365	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
366	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
367	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
368	\end{lemma}
369	
370	\begin{proof}
371	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
372	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
373	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
374	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
375	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
376	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
377	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
378	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
379	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
380	\end{proof}

382	\begin{lemma}
383	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
384	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
385	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
386	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
387	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
388	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
389	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
390	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
391	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
392	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
393	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
394	  \[
395	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
396	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
397	    \;=\;
398	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
399	  \]
400	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
401	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
402	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
403	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
404	\end{lemma}
405	
406	\begin{proof}
407	  Proved directly in Lean.
408	\end{proof}

410	\begin{lemma}
411	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
412	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
413	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
414	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
415	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
416	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
417	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
418	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
419	  collapses to the transport-light form
420	  \[
421	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
422	      \;\circ\; e_2
423	    \;=\;
424	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
425	      \;\circ\; e_3,
426	  \]
427	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
428	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
429	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
430	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
431	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
432	  composition (pentagon) law.
433	\end{lemma}
434	
435	\begin{proof}
436	  Proved directly in Lean.
437	\end{proof}

445	  \[
446	    G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
447	  \]

715	\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}
716	
717	The comparison is proved by the \emph{acyclic-resolution} (Cartan--Leray) route. The
718	augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) whose terms are
719	acyclic for \(f_*\); the abstract homological-algebra
720	Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then identifies the derived
721	functor with the cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\). The two
722	geometric inputs are isolated in the following sub-lemmas.

724	\begin{lemma}

732	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We are asked to show that the extended complex
733	  % \begin{equation}
734	  % \label{equation-extended}
735	  % 0 \to
736	  % M \to
737	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
738	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
739	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to

748	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof).}
749	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
750	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with all
751	  intersections affine. Form the augmented {\v C}ech complex on \(X\),
752	  \[
753	    0 \;\to\; \mathcal{F} \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^0 \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^1 \;\to\;
754	    \mathcal{C}^2 \;\to\; \cdots,
755	    \qquad

757	      (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*\bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
758	  \]

761	  \(\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0\). This augmented complex is exact; that is, the
762	  {\v C}ech nerve is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).

768	  checked on the stalks, equivalently over each affine open of a basis. Restricting
769	  to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) on which \(\mathcal{F}|_U =
770	  \widetilde{M}\) and over which the cover restricts to a standard cover \(U =
771	  \bigcup D(f_i)\), the augmented {\v C}ech complex becomes the extended complex of
772	  localisations

780	\begin{lemma}
781	[Presheaf description of the higher direct images]
782	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}
783	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_isSheafify_presheafCohomology}
784	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
785	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01XJ, Lemma
786	  % \texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images} (the higher direct images are the
787	  % sheafifications of the cohomology presheaves of preimages) (read from
788	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L591--603).
789	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces.
790	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
791	  % The sheaves $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ are the sheaves
792	  % associated to the presheaves
793	  % $$
794	  % V \longmapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F})
795	  % $$
796	  % with restriction mappings as in Equation (\ref{equation-restriction-mapping}).
797	  % There is a similar statement for $R^if_*$ applied to a
798	  % bounded below complex $\mathcal{F}^\bullet$."
799	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01XJ
800	  (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}).}
801	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{G}\) an
802	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For every \(k \geq 0\) the higher direct image
803	  \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G} = (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{G})\) is the sheaf
804	  on \(S\) associated to the presheaf
805	  \[
806	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
807	    \qquad V \subseteq S \text{ open},
808	  \]
809	  with the evident restriction maps. In particular \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) vanishes
810	  iff \(H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for every \(V\) in a basis of
811	  the topology of \(S\); since the affine opens form such a basis, it suffices to
812	  check the vanishing on affine \(V\). This is the local-on-the-base description
813	  through which \(f_*\)-acyclicity is reduced to absolute cohomology vanishing on
814	  preimages.
815	\end{lemma}
816	
817	\begin{proof}
818	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
819	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
820	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. By definition \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is the \(k\)-th
821	  cohomology sheaf of the complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\); since the abelian
822	  structure on \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules computes cokernels and kernels objectwise on
823	  the level of presheaves and then sheafifies, that cohomology sheaf is the
824	  sheafification of the presheaf \(V \mapsto H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)
825	  = H^k\bigl(\mathcal{I}^\bullet(f^{-1}(V))\bigr) = H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\),
826	  the last equality because \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is an injective (hence
827	  acyclic) resolution computing the absolute cohomology of \(f^{-1}(V)\). Evaluation
828	  of a sheafification vanishes exactly when the underlying presheaf vanishes on a basis,
829	  giving the stated affine-local criterion.
830	\end{proof}

856	  \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}, and Cohomology,
857	  \texttt{lemma-relative-Leray}.}
858	  Let \(X\) be separated, \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\) the open immersion of an affine
859	  open \(U\), and \(f : X \to S\) any morphism, with composite \(g := f \circ j : U
860	  \to S\). Then:
861	  \begin{enumerate}
862	    \item \(j\) is an affine morphism (an open immersion of an affine open into a
863	      separated scheme), so its higher direct images vanish: \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} = 0\)
864	      for all \(q \geq 1\) and every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module
865	      \(\mathcal{H}\); and
866	    \item consequently, for every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{H}\) on \(U\) and every
867	      \(k \geq 0\),
868	      \[
869	        R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr) \;\cong\; R^k g_*\mathcal{H}.
870	      \]
871	  \end{enumerate}

877	  affine morphism: separatedness makes the immersion \(j\) quasi-separated and the
878	  preimage of any affine open of \(X\) meets \(U\) in an affine, so \(j\) is affine.
879	  The relative affine vanishing for affine morphisms then gives \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} =
880	  0\) for \(q \geq 1\); concretely, by the presheaf description
881	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}) \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H}\) is the
882	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^q(j^{-1}(V), \mathcal{H})\), and for affine \(V\) the
883	  preimage \(j^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which the Serre vanishing of
884	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} kills \(H^q\) for \(q \geq 1\).
885	
886	  (2) Apply the relative Leray spectral sequence to the composition \(g = f \circ j\):
887	  it has second page \(E_2^{p, q} = R^p f_*\bigl(R^q j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr)\) and

891	  R^k f_*\bigl(R^0 j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr) \cong R^k g_*\mathcal{H}\) for every \(k\).

994	  (\texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}) and
995	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}.}
996	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism of schemes, let
997	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let
998	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover of
999	  \(X\) (so, by separatedness, every intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is
1000	  affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative {\v C}ech complex
1001	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1002	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} compute the higher direct images: for every
1003	  \(i \geq 0\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules
1004	  \[
1005	    H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
1006	      \;\cong\; R^i f_*\mathcal{F}.
1007	  \]
1008	  In particular, over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), taking global
1009	  sections gives \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
1010	  H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\) as \(A\)-modules for all \(i\).
1011	
1012	  Two points about the precise form of the comparison should be noted. First, the
1013	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) on the right-hand side
1014	  is the one obtained from injective resolutions, which is available only when the
1015	  category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has enough injectives; accordingly this
1016	  comparison is asserted under the hypothesis that injective resolutions exist in
1017	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\), and it compares the (always-defined) {\v C}ech higher
1018	  direct image \(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}))\)
1019	  against the derived-functor one only in that case. Second, we assert the comparison in the
1020	  weak form of the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism, i.e.\ as
1021	  \(\bigl(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})) \cong
1022	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr)\) without singling out a chosen natural isomorphism.
1023	  This existence statement is all the downstream consumers (representability of the
1024	  relative Picard functor, local triviality of the comparison) require: they use
1025	  only that the unconditional {\v C}ech \(R^i f_*\) and the derived-functor
1026	  \(R^i f_*\) agree as objects, never a specific identification, so committing to a
1027	  canonical natural transformation would add coherence obligations that play no
1028	  role in the applications.
1029	\end{lemma}
1030

1032	% Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
1033	% this is a special case of
1034	% Cohomology, Lemma
1035	% \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence-application}."

1038	  The source proves the statement by exhibiting it, in view of Serre vanishing on
1039	  affines, as a special case of the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral-sequence
1040	  criterion. We organise the same fact as the standard \emph{acyclic-resolution}
1041	  (Cartan--Leray) argument, which needs no spectral sequence beyond the single
1042	  abstract homological-algebra Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}.
1043	
1044	  Apply the abstract lemma with the right-derivable additive functor \(G = f_*\)
1045	  (the pushforward \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X) \to \operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)), the object
1046	  \(A = \mathcal{F}\), and the resolution \(J^\bullet = \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) given by
1047	  the {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}. Its two hypotheses are
1048	  exactly the geometric sub-lemmas above:
1049	  \begin{enumerate}
1050	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_augmented_resolution} the augmented {\v C}ech
1051	      complex \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0 \to \mathcal{C}^1 \to \cdots\) is
1052	      exact, i.e.\ \(\mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in
1053	      \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
1054	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic} each term
1055	      \(\mathcal{C}^p\) is right \(f_*\)-acyclic:
1056	      \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
1057	  \end{enumerate}
1058	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then gives, for every \(i\), an
1059	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th derived functor evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\) and
1060	  the \(i\)-th cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the
1061	  resolution,
1062	  \[
1063	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})
1064	      \;\cong\;
1065	    H^i\bigl(f_*\,\mathcal{C}^\bullet\bigr).
1066	  \]
1067	  By construction (Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}) the complex \(f_*\,
1068	  \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is precisely the relative {\v C}ech complex
1069	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so its \(i\)-th
1070	  cohomology is \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})
1071	  \bigr)\); and \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})\) is the
1072	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) of
1073	  Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}. This yields the isomorphism
1074	  \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr) \cong
1075	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) asserted by the lemma; taking it in the existence
1076	  (\(\operatorname{Nonempty}\)) form discards the choice of resolution, as required.

1078	  recovers \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S,

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, definition of $R^i f_*$ as the
5	% $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex
6	% and references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter records the derived-functor higher direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\)
11	for \(i \geq 1\). It provides the single object that the {\v C}ech computation of
12	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} compares against: the comparison
13	theorem \(\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}\) identifies the cohomology of the
14	relative {\v C}ech complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) wherever the derived functor
15	is defined.
16	
17	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category).
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
19	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	
21	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
22	
23	\begin{definition}\leanok
24	[Higher direct image]
25	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
26	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
27	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
28	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
29	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
30	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
31	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
32	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
33	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
34	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
35	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
36	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
37	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
38	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
39	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
40	  % the sheaf
41	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
42	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
43	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
44	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
45	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
46	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
47	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
48	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
49	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
50	  sheafification of the presheaf
51	  \[
52	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
53	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
54	  \]
55	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
56	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
57	\end{definition}
